name: Greater Swiss Mountain Dog
description: The Greater Swiss Mountain Dog is the largest and oldest of the four Swiss Sennenhund breeds, believed to descend from the mastiff-type dogs brought to Switzerland by Julius Caesar's legions over 2,000 years ago and later used extensively as draft animals, herders, and farm guardians by Swiss farmers. Unlike its cousin the Bernese Mountain Dog, the Greater Swiss has a short, dense coat that is considerably lower maintenance, though it sheds year-round. A lick-fit syndrome — involuntary swallowing and grass-eating episodes related to esophageal issues — affects some individuals and can be distressing to observe before diagnosis and management.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Elbow Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Elbow dysplasia is an umbrella term covering several developmental abnormalities of the elbow joint including fragmented coronoid process, osteochondritis dissecans, and ununited anconeal process. It causes foreleg lameness and arthritis.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Surgical intervention is often recommended to remove bone fragments or reshape joint surfaces. Medical management includes NSAIDs, joint supplements, and physiotherapy.
prevention_tips: Control growth rate in large breeds

conditions_detail.name: Epilepsy
conditions_detail.description: Epilepsy is the most common neurological disorder in dogs, characterised by recurrent seizures. Idiopathic (genetic) epilepsy is the most frequent form in young to middle-aged dogs with no underlying structural brain lesion.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Anticonvulsant drugs (phenobarbital, potassium bromide, levetiracetam) control seizures in the majority of cases. Treatment is lifelong. Cluster seizures and status epilepticus require emergency treatment.
prevention_tips: DNA testing available for several breeds
Avoid breeding affected dogs with idiopathic epilepsy
